facts_disclosed: Cutting back on work -- wants to enjoy life more

turns.content: I've been on the bench for eighteen years. That's a long time to carry other people's worst days on your shoulders. I'm starting to think about what the next chapter looks like.

Eighteen years is a significant tenure, and carrying the weight of people's most difficult moments for that long takes a real toll -- even when you're deeply committed to the work. Are you thinking about retirement, or more of a gradual shift in how much you take on?

Not retirement. I'm not ready for that and honestly I don't think the bench is ready for me to leave yet. But I've been talking with Carlos -- my husband -- and we both feel the same way. We want to travel more. We want to actually enjoy life while we're still healthy enough to do it. So I'm thinking about cutting back. Fewer cases, more living.

That's a really thoughtful middle ground -- staying engaged in work you clearly care about while creating space for everything else. "While we're still healthy enough" is the key phrase there. People who wait for the perfect retirement moment sometimes find it arrives too late to enjoy fully.

When you say cutting back, what does that look like practically in your position? Can you control your caseload, or does that depend on how the court assigns cases?

There's some flexibility, particularly for senior judges. I can request a reduced docket. It wouldn't happen overnight, but the chief judge and I have a good relationship and I think the conversation would be well received. The younger judges need more courtroom time anyway. It would be good for everyone.

That sounds like you've already thought through the institutional logic, which probably makes the conversation easier -- you're not just asking for a personal favor, you're proposing something that benefits the bench as a whole. Framing it as making room for the younger judges is smart and generous.

A few things that might be worth thinking through before you have that conversation:

- **What reduced actually means to you** -- a specific percentage reduction? Certain days off? Avoiding certain case types? Having a clear picture helps.
- **Timeline** -- when do you want this to start? Having a target date makes it concrete rather than aspirational.
- **Financial impact** -- does a reduced docket affect your compensation, pension accrual, or benefits? Worth knowing the numbers before you commit.

Do you have a sense of what the ideal schedule would look like?

I've thought about three days a week on the bench instead of five. That gives me two full days for Carlos and me to plan trips, spend time with family, actually breathe. I've been running at full speed since I was appointed and before that I was running at full speed as a litigator. At some point you have to ask yourself what all of it is for.

Three days is a meaningful reduction that still keeps you active and contributing, while creating real space -- not token space. Two full days a week adds up to a hundred free days a year. That's transformative.

And your question -- "what all of it is for" -- is one that a lot of high-achieving professionals avoid asking until they're forced to by health or circumstances. The fact that you're asking it while you're still healthy and choosing to act on it is a strength, not a retreat.

Where does Carlos want to travel? Do you two have a list, or is it more of a general desire that hasn't been planned yet?

Oh, Carlos has a list. That man has had a list for years. Spain is at the top -- we want to do a proper trip, not a rushed tourist thing. Barcelona, Andalusia, maybe the Basque country. My family is originally from Cuba but the roots before that go back to Spain, so there's a personal dimension to it. And then he wants to do Portugal, Argentina, maybe even Japan. He's ambitious.

Carlos sounds like he's been quietly building this itinerary while waiting for you to say the word. Spain with the family connection sounds especially meaningful -- tracing roots through Andalusia is a particular kind of travel that hits differently than just sightseeing.

Practical thought: if you move to three days a week, you could structure longer trips around court recesses and still take extended weekends throughout the year. A two-week Spain trip during summer recess plus several long weekends for shorter trips would let you cover a lot of ground without ever feeling like you're abandoning your responsibilities.

Have you started the conversation with the chief judge yet, or are you still in the deciding phase?

Still deciding. Or maybe I've decided and I'm just building up to it. Eighteen years of being the person everyone depends on -- it's hard to say I need to step back even when I know it's the right thing. There's a part of me that feels like I'm letting people down.

That feeling is real and it's worth naming, but I'd push back on the framing. Reducing your docket isn't letting people down -- it's ensuring that the cases you do take get the best version of you, not the depleted version. A judge running on empty for twenty-five years serves no one as well as a judge who chose sustainability at eighteen.

And the people who depend on you most -- Carlos, your family -- have been sharing you with the court for nearly two decades. Giving them more of your time isn't selfish. It's overdue.

The guilt you're feeling is probably a sign that you've been in service mode for so long that it feels like your identity. Cutting back doesn't change who you are. It changes your schedule.

You know, when you put it that way -- the best version of me versus the depleted version -- that's exactly it. I've been depleted for a while and pretending otherwise. Carlos sees it. I see it. I think I needed someone to tell me that stepping back is not the same as stepping down.

It's not. Stepping back is strategic. Stepping down is an ending. What you're describing is a recalibration -- you're choosing to do the work differently so you can keep doing it well and also have a life beyond it.

When you're ready to have the conversation with the chief judge, keep it simple: you've served eighteen years, you want to continue contributing, you believe a reduced docket benefits the bench, and you'd like to discuss a three-day arrangement. You don't need to justify wanting to enjoy your life. That's not a weakness in the argument -- it's the whole point.
Claro. You're right. I'm going to talk to Carlos tonight and then I'll schedule the meeting. It's time. Thank you -- this was more helpful than you might realize.

I'm glad it helped. Eighteen years of service earns you the right to design the next chapter on your terms. Go talk to Carlos -- I suspect he'll have the Spain itinerary pulled up within five minutes.
